skill.name: rust-async-patterns
skill.description: Guide async Rust development with Tokio, channels, streams, async traits, error handling, graceful shutdown, and debugging patterns.
skill.summary: Apply production Rust async patterns for Tokio services and concurrent systems.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported process spawning, backtick execution, network, reconnaissance, weak crypto, and obfuscation patterns in SKILL.md. Manual review found these are Markdown, Rust documentation examples, Tokio task scheduling, resource links, and SQL examples, not executable marketplace behavior. No prompt injection attempt, data exfiltration, hidden commands, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Tokio Task Spawning Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged spawn calls as external command execution. These locations use tokio::spawn or JoinSet::spawn inside Rust examples to schedule async tasks, not to start operating system processes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Rust async task examples and do not invoke shell, process, or command APIs. No user-controlled command string or process execution path exists in the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks and Code Fences
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution. The locations are Markdown code fences, inline Rust terms, headings, or prose delimiters in SKILL.md, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious characters are Markdown formatting around documentation examples. SKILL.md is not Ruby or shell source, and the backticks do not define executable behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network References in Examples and Resources
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged network indicators. The reviewed lines contain a placeholder example URL, a reqwest error type, and public documentation links, with no hidden request logic or exfiltration path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are documentation-only examples and external learning resources. No code in the skill performs a network request or transmits user data.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Heuristic Matches for Obfuscation, Weak Crypto, and Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged front matter, description text, SQL examples, and tracing instrumentation as obfuscation, weak crypto, or system reconnaissance. Manual review found no encoded payload, cryptographic implementation, system probing, or prompt injection text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are YAML front matter, parameterized SQL snippets, and tracing labels. They do not contain malicious instructions, obfuscated code, weak cryptographic code, or host reconnaissance commands.

content.value_statement: Async Rust code can fail through blocking calls, unbounded tasks, unclear cancellation, and weak error handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Tokio patterns for concurrent services.
actual_capabilities: Explains Tokio task spawning, JoinSet usage, and bounded concurrent execution.
Shows channel patterns with mpsc, broadcast, oneshot, and watch channels.
Covers async error handling with anyhow, thiserror, timeouts, and custom errors.
Demonstrates graceful shutdown with CancellationToken, signals, and broadcast channels.
Documents async traits, stream processing, shared state, and resource pool patterns.
Provides debugging guidance with tracing instrumentation and tokio-console.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run, test, or modify Rust projects by itself.
Examples use placeholder types and functions that require adaptation before compiling.
It focuses on Tokio and does not deeply cover async-std or smol runtimes.
It does not provide a full security review for user applications or services.

use_cases.title: Design a Tokio service architecture
use_cases.description: Plan task structure, shutdown handling, channels, and timeout boundaries for a new async Rust service.

use_cases.title: Review async Rust code for reliability
use_cases.description: Check existing code for blocking calls, unbounded spawning, lock misuse, missing cancellation, and weak error handling.

use_cases.title: Learn practical async patterns
use_cases.description: Study common Tokio examples for tasks, channels, streams, traits, resource management, and debugging workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the async model
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the Rust async execution model using this skill. Focus on Future, await, tasks, runtimes, and wakers.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a channel pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: I need async communication between Rust tasks. Compare mpsc, broadcast, oneshot, and watch for my use case.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Tokio workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Tokio service design for task spawning, bounded concurrency, cancellation, timeouts, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design shutdown and observability
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production async Rust shutdown and debugging strategy using CancellationToken, tracing, JoinSet, and tokio-console.

output_examples.input: Review my async worker pool design for reliability.
output_examples.output: A structured review that flags unbounded spawning, missing cancellation, timeout gaps, lock usage risks, and tracing improvements.

output_examples.input: Help me choose the right Tokio channel.
output_examples.output: A recommendation that maps each communication need to mpsc, broadcast, oneshot, or watch with tradeoffs and failure modes.

output_examples.input: Plan graceful shutdown for my Rust service.
output_examples.output: A shutdown design covering signal handling, cancellation propagation, task cleanup, timeout limits, and observability checkpoints.

best_practices: Use bounded concurrency with JoinSet, buffers, or semaphores when work can grow with input size.
Propagate cancellation and timeouts across task boundaries instead of relying on process termination.
Instrument async work with tracing so task behavior can be debugged under load.
anti_patterns: Do not use blocking operations inside async tasks unless they are isolated with appropriate blocking APIs.
Do not hold locks across await points when the locked value can be needed by other tasks.
Do not spawn tasks without ownership, error handling, cancellation, or concurrency limits.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Rust code?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill that provides async Rust patterns and review guidance.

faq.question: Which runtime does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Tokio, including tasks, channels, signals, timers, tracing, and tokio-console.

faq.question: Can it help with async trait design?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes async trait examples using async-trait and repository-style abstractions.

faq.question: Does it cover database or HTTP implementation details?
faq.answer: Only at a pattern level. The examples use placeholder database and network concepts.

faq.question: Is it useful for production services?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes bounded concurrency, error propagation, graceful shutdown, tracing, and resource management.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it during code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives concrete async Rust checks for reviewing Tokio services and concurrent workflows.
